title: Electrochemical properties of octakis(hydroxyethylthio)-substituted phthalocyanines
abstract:
Electrochemical properties of octakis(hydroxyethylthio)-
substituted phthalocyanines
A. R. O¨ ZKAYA1, E. HAMURYUDAN2, Z. A. BAYIR2 and O¨ . BEKAROG˘LU2*
1Department of Chemistry, Atatu¨rk Faculty of Education, Marmara University, Kadiko¨y, Istanbul, Turkey
2Department of Chemistry, Technical University of Istanbul, Maslak, Istanbul, Turkey
Received 23 July 1999; Revised 18 November 1999
Accepted 25 November 1999
ABSTRACT: Cyclic voltammetry of octakis(hydroxyethylthio)-substituted phthalocyanines with 2H (H2OHETPc),
Zn(II) (ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2)) and Co(II) (CoII(OHETPc(ÿ2)) in the phthalocyanine core has been investigated in
dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), and some of the results were evaluated together with their absorption spectra in the
visible range. The redox processes due to both aggregated and non-aggregated species were observed on the
voltammograms of phthalocyanines with Zn(II). The effect of chloride ion on the cyclic voltammetry of
CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) has been investigated in the DMSO/ClO4
ÿ couple. The comparison of cyclic voltammetry for
CoIIOHETPc in the absence and presence of chloride ion showed that chloride ion does not bind to either Co(II) or
Co(I) but does bind very strongly to Co(III). The detailed electrochemistry of CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) was investigated in
terms of the Co(I)/Co(II) and Co(II)/Co(III) redox couples, and various equilibria related to the species coordinated to
Co(II) and Co(III) in the phthalocyanine core. Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
KEYWORDS: phthalocyanines; thiaethers; pentanuclear complex; cyclic voltammetry
INTRODUCTION
Metallophthalocyanine (MPc) complexes exhibit a rich
electrochemical behaviour due to the accessibility of a range
of oxidation states centred on the Pc unit or on the central
metal [1–3]. Some MPc systems bind one or two axial
ligands; such coordination can have a major effect upon the
observed redox activity [4–10]. On the other hand, many
phthalocyanines (Pcs) may aggregate to a greater or lesser
extent, and aggregation can inﬂuence the observed redox
potentials [11–13]. However, most unsubstituted MPc
species have only very limited solubility in virtually all
solvents, thereby limiting solution phase redox measure-
ments. The solubility of Pcs can be improved by introducing
substituents in the periphery which increase the distance
between the planar macrocycle rings carrying the p-
electrons and make the solvation easier. Recently, new
Pcs with octakis(hydroxyethylthio) substituents have been
synthesized
through
cyclotetramerization
of
1,2-bis-
(hydroxyethylthio)-4,5-dicyanobenzene
[14].
In
this
work the redox properties of octakis(hydroxyethylthio)-
substituted Pcs with 2H, Zn(II) and Co(II) (H2OHETPc,
ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) and CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2)) (Fig. 1) have
been investigated by cyclic voltammetry in DMSO, and
some of the results were evaluated together with their
absorption spectra in the visible range.
EXPERIMENTAL
The ﬁrst step in the synthetic procedure was to obtain the
phthalonitrile derivative containing the thioether group; this
reaction was carried out at 50°C in dry DMF with Na2CO3
as the base. The metal-free phthalocyanine was obtained
directly by the reaction of phthalonitrile in hydroquinone as
a uniphase fused melt. The metal phthalocyanines were
synthesized by using the anhydrous metal salts (CoCl2,
Zn(O2CMe)2). As a high-boiling aprotic solvent, quinoline
was the solvent of choice for the preparation of phthalo-
cyaninatozinc(II), whereas cyclotetramerization of phthalo-
nitrile to the Co(II) derivative was carried out in ethylene
glycol. The puriﬁcation steps required a ﬁnal column
chromatography on silica gel [14].
Cyclic voltammetry was performed with a Princeton
Applied Research (PAR) Model 273 potentiostat controlled
by an external computer. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs)
were collected with the usual three-electrode set-up and
recorded using an X–Y recorder (RE 0091). For cyclic
voltammetry a platinum wire served as the counter electrode
and a commercial saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was
employed as the reference electrode. The working electrode
was a platinum plate (0.64 cm2), polished with a water
suspension of Al2O3 before each experiment. The last
polishing was done with a particle size of 50 nm. The SCE
was separated from the solution by a double bridge
containing saturated KCl adjacent to the SCE, and DMSO
and carrier adjacent to the solution in order to minimize
liquid–liquid junction potentials.
Controlled potential electrolysis was performed at the
constant potentials corresponding to the voltammetric peak
Journal of Porphyrins and Phthalocyanines
J. Porphyrins Phthalocyanines 4, 689–697 (2000)
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
———————
*Correspondence
to: O¨ . Bekarog˘lu, Department
of Chemistry,
Technical University of Istanbul, Maslak, Istanbul, Turkey.
 potentials of the phthalocyanines using platinum gauze
working and counter electrodes and an SCE.
DMSO, dried over molecular sieves, was used as the
solvent in the experiments. Electrochemical-grade tetra-
butylammonium perchlorate (TBAP) was employed as the
supporting electrolyte in 0.1 mol dmÿ3 concentration.
Electronic spectra were recorded with a Unicam UV2
UV-vis spectrometer.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
For all the Pcs used in this study, voltammetric data,
determined using a platinum working electrode in DMSO at
a scan rate v of 50 mV sÿ1, are shown in Table 1.
Two reversible one-electron reductions were observed at
E1/2 = ÿ0.74 V and E1/2 = ÿ1.11 V for ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2)
(couples II and III respectively) as shown in Fig. 2. The

For all the Pcs used in this study, voltammetric data,
determined using a platinum working electrode in DMSO at
a scan rate v of 50 mV sÿ1, are shown in Table 1.
Two reversible one-electron reductions were observed at
E1/2 = ÿ0.74 V and E1/2 = ÿ1.11 V for ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2)
(couples II and III respectively) as shown in Fig. 2. The
Table 1. Voltammetric data for octakis(hydroxyethylthio)-
substituted phthalocyanines at a platinum electrode at
Fig. 2. Cyclic voltammogram of 1.2  10ÿ3 mol dmÿ3 Zn(II)OHETPc(ÿ2) in DMSO/TBAP (0.1 mol dmÿ3) at 50 mV sÿ1.

difference between the ﬁrst and second reduction processes
of the complex is 0.37 V, which compares to an average
separation for the ﬁrst and second reduction processes of
various phthalocyaninatozinc complexes at the Pc ring
system [13, 15–19]. Thus these two couples are assigned to
successive ring reduction processes, namely ZnIIPc(ÿ2)/
[ZnIIPc(ÿ3)]ÿ and [ZnIIPc(ÿ3)]ÿ/[ZnIIPc(ÿ4)]2ÿ respec-
ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) displayed an irreversible cathodic wave
(III') (Fig. 2) without the corresponding anodic wave, which
was not observed even with the potential scan reversed
immediately after III'. This cathodic peak cannot be
attributed to the reduction process of Pc(ÿ4)/Pc(ÿ5),
because the second and third ring reduction potentials of
various phthalocyaninatozinc complexes are separated by
about 0.55 V [20]. It probably results from impurities in the
ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) appears to be aggregated in DMSO
solution as monitored by the band at 637 nm in the UV-vis
spectrum of the complex (Fig. 3). If there is an equilibrium
between the aggregated and non-aggregated species and if
the aggregation–disaggregation equilibrium is slow rela-
tive to the electrochemical timescale, redox process due to
both aggregated and non-aggregated species can simulta-
neously be observed [13]. As shown in Scheme 1, there
should be an equilibrium between aggregated and non-
However, most of the ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) species are
probably aggregated and the equilibrium B should be very
facile towards the left because of the high aggregation
tendency. Under such circumstances the ﬁrst reduction of
ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) is expected to show only one wave (II)
corresponding to the reduction of aggregated species as
observed in Fig. 2. On the other hand, monoanion species
produced by the ﬁrst reduction process (II) probably
disaggregate owing to their negative charge and convert to
monomer species immediately after the ﬁrst reduction
There are additionally two redox couples (I and I')
between 0.60 and 0.85 V (Fig. 2, Table 1). The potential
difference between I'a and Ia is only 0.130 V, so Ia cannot
be the second-step Pc ring oxidation. There are only a few
reports [15, 21–23] which give a potential value for the
second-step ring oxidation of zinc phthalocyanines. In those
studies the difference between the ﬁrst and second ring
oxidations was reported as about 0.7 V. Therefore I'a and Ia
in Fig. 2 should be assigned to the oxidation of aggregated
and non-aggregated ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) species as shown in
Scheme 1 in spite of the previous conclusion that most of the
ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) species are probably aggregated. Also,
there should be an equilibrium between the aggregated and
non-aggregated [ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ1)] monocation species,
since Ic is observed during the reverse cathodic scan.
Aggregation appears to be inﬂuenced by the net charge on
the MPc unit, being more important with positively charged
than negatively charged species.
Owing to the high residual current, it was not possible to
determine exactly the number of electrons involved in the
last reduction process of all the complexes used in this work.
However, the controlled potential electrolysis of each
complex showed that the last reduction process is likely to
involve one electron. Compared with the values in the
literature [20], the peak potentials for this process of
H2OHETPc(ÿ2) and ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) shown in Table 1
are indicative of a redox process corresponding to the
With the exception of Pc ring oxidation, cyclic volt-
ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) (Fig. 4). The ﬁrst ring reduction of
H2OHETPc occurs at a potential (E1/2 = ÿ0.58 V) 0.16 V
ÿ0.74 V) (Table 1). The ﬁrst ring oxidation is separated
Fig. 3. Electronic spectrum of Zn(II)OHETPc(ÿ2) (1  10ÿ5 mol dmÿ3).

J. Porphyrins Phthalocyanines 4, 689–697 (2000)
OCTAKIS(HYDROXYETHYLTHIO)-SUBSTITUTED PHTHALOCYANINES
from the ﬁrst ring reduction by approximately 1.5–1.6 V for
metal-free and main group phthalocyanines, which corre-
sponds to the magnitude of energy difference between the
highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest
unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [24, 25]. However,
the individual potentials for the ﬁrst ring reduction and the
ﬁrst ring oxidation do vary considerably and are functions of
the polarizing power of the central metal ion. Since the ﬁrst
reduction of H2OHETPc occurs at a potential less negative
than that of ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2), the oxidation of metal-free
Pc is expected to occur at a more positive potential with
respect to ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2). Therefore, in contrast to
Fig. 4. Cyclic voltammogram of 2  10ÿ4 mol dmÿ3 H2OHETPc in DMSO/TBAP (0.1 mol dmÿ3) at 50 mV sÿ1.
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
J. Porphyrins Phthalocyanines 4, 689–697 (2000)
ZnIIOHETPc, it was not possible to observe the oxidation of
H2OHETPc, which probably occurs at a more positive
potential than the solvent-limited potential in the experi-
mental conditions of our work.
The electrochemistry of cobalt(II) phthalocyanines is
split into two sections, that referring to donor solvents and
that referring to non-donor solvents [26–29]. Whether the
metal or the ring is oxidized ﬁrst depending on the nature of
the solvent is the principle difference for the redox couples
of cobalt(II) phthalocyanines. Donor solvents (such as
dimethylsulfoxide, dimethylformamide and acetonitrile) or
coordinating counter anions in non-donor solvents (such as
strongly favour CoIIIPc by coordinating along the axis to
form six-coordinate L2CoIIIPc species. On the other hand,
oxidation to Co(III) is inhibited in the absence of strong
donor molecules and ring oxidation occurs ﬁrst. Figure 5(A)
shows the CV of CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) in the DMSO/TBAP
electrolyte system. While V and VI correspond to the redox
processes of [CoIIIOHETPc(ÿ2)]/CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) and
CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2)/CoIOHETPc(ÿ2)]ÿ respectively, VII is
assigned to the ﬁrst ring reduction of [CoIOHETPc(ÿ2)]ÿ/
[CoIOHETPc(ÿ3)] [26]. Since cobalt(II) is reduced ﬁrst in
donor solvents, the process VIII should correspond to the
reduction couple of Pc(ÿ3)/Pc(ÿ4). The redox potentials of
Fig. 5. Cyclic voltammetry of 3.9  10ÿ4 mol dmÿ3 Co(II)OHETPc(ÿ2) in DMSO/TBAP (0.1 mol dmÿ3) at a Pt electrode, at varying
scan rates and switching potentials. (A) Scan rate, 100 mV sÿ1; cathodic limit, ÿ2 V; anodic limit, 0.9 V. (B) Scan rates from lower to
upper curves are 25, 50, 100 and 250 mV sÿ1 respectively; cathodic limit, ÿ0.7 V; anodic limit, 0.75 V.
OHETPc(ÿ2) redox couple II in DMSO solution at various
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
J. Porphyrins Phthalocyanines 4, 689–697 (2000)
OCTAKIS(HYDROXYETHYLTHIO)-SUBSTITUTED PHTHALOCYANINES
CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) measured in this work are consistent with
those reported in the literature [20].
The [CoIIIOHETPc(ÿ2)]/CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) process (la-
belled Va, Vc in Fig. 5(B)) showed a scan rate-dependent ic/
ia ratio approaching unity at higher scan rates (Table 2). This
behaviour should be related to a change in the groups axially
coordinated to the cobalt upon oxidation. The bulk solute in
CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) based upon earlier studies [26, 30, 31].
Since the axial site is likely to be labile, there will be other
species in equilibrium. The most important are likely to be
These species are expected to have three different Co(II)/
Co(III) oxidation potentials. However, if the equilibria are
fast relative to the voltammetric timescale, only the
oxidation of the most easily oxidized species should be
observed. The most easily oxidized species should be E
because of the net negative charge on the molecule [27].
Therefore the scan rate dependence of process V can be
interpreted in terms of the processes
The ﬁve-coordinate species J produced by equilibrium (3)
is expected to be much easier to reduce than the six-
coordinate species F and thus has a redox potential positive
of couple V. Therefore E is oxidized to F at peak Va (Fig.
5(B)). F converts to G at least to a small degree, and G is
spontaneously reduced on the positive side of peak Va
since its reduction potential lies positive of Va. Equili-
Scheme 2. Hydroxyethylthio substituents are omitted for clarity.
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
J. Porphyrins Phthalocyanines 4, 689–697 (2000)
brium (3) is driven to the right by this reduction process
and therefore decreases the intensity of the current at peak
Vc. Although the ﬁve-coordinate Co(III) species is likely
to be formed in only minute amounts, equilibrium (3)
should be sufﬁciently facile that, during the time of the
cathodic sweep positive of couple V, some of the Co(III)
species on the electrode will be reduced and therefore not
contribute current to cathodic wave Vc. Previous studies
[32] show that although axial substitution of six-coordinate
Co(III) species is usually very slow, axial sites on Co(III)
macrocycles are more labile. At higher scan rates there is
less time for the rearrangement to occur and more
reversible behaviour is observed. We note that, at a given
perchlorate ion concentration (Table 2), supporting the
view that species E is involved as proposed.
Peak VIc was observed to become more rounded relative
to VIa as the scan rate was increased (Fig. 5(B)). This
behaviour may also be explained in terms of the presence of
different species (equilibria (1)). If the equilibria are fast
relative to the voltammetric timescale, species D and E
rearrange to C during the ﬁrst reduction process, to a great
extent, and thus at low scan rates peak VIc should involve
only the reduction of C, which is expected to reduce more
easily than D and E. At higher scan rates there is less time
for the rearrangement of species D and E to C to occur and
peak VIc should involve the reduction of C, D and E.
Therefore cathodic wave VIc becomes more rounded at high
scan rates, since these species are expected to have slightly
different reduction potentials.
represented by Scheme 2, where V–VIII correspond to the
peaks in Fig. 5 and hydroxyethylthio substituents are
Cyclic Voltammetry of CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) in the
Figures 6(A)–6(E) reveal the effect upon the cyclic
voltammetry of CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) as chloride ion is added
in molar excess relative to the solute. It is clearly seen that
the cathodic portion of couple V (Vc) disappears, while the
response becomes irreversible and a new reduction peak
(VC) appears at a less positive potential of peak (Vc). The
new reduction peak VC may be attributed to impurities in
the sample materials. In the meantime the anodic portion of
peak V (Va) is not lost, but decreases in height when the Clÿ
concentration is higher than 8  10ÿ4mol dmÿ3. Both the
anodic and cathodic portions of peak VI do not disappear in
the chloride ion-containing solution, but show an equivalent
decrease in height to that of Va at Clÿ concentrations higher
than 8  10ÿ4moldmÿ3. From these observations it can be
concluded that chloride ion does not bind to either,
CoIIPc(ÿ2) or [CoIPc(ÿ2)]ÿ, but does bind very strongly
to [CoIIIPc(ÿ2)]. The fact that the potential of the Co(II)/
Co(I) couple is not affected by chloride ion (Fig. 6) provides
strong evidence for this conclusion.
In a previous report [9] the cyclic voltammetry of
was studied in dimethylformamide and the effect of
hydroxide ions on the redox processes was investigated.
Although the results of our study are generally similar to
those of the previous report, there are also important
differences, probably due to the different coordinating
powers of DMF/OHÿ and DMSO/Clÿ couples. In the
previous study [9] it was reported that when hydroxide ion is
added to a DMF solution of CoIIPc(ÿ2) under an inert
atmosphere, the solution is converted, with the time
Fig. 6. Cyclic voltammetry of 3.9  10ÿ4 mol dmÿ3 Co(II)OHETPc(ÿ2) in DMSO/TBAP (0.1 mol dmÿ3)/TMACl at a Pt electrode.
Concentration of TMACl: (A) 0, (B) 3.3  10ÿ4, (C) 8.0  10ÿ4, (D) 1.4  10ÿ3 and (E) 2.0  10ÿ3 mol dmÿ3.
Copyright # 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
J. Porphyrins Phthalocyanines 4, 689–697 (2000)
OCTAKIS(HYDROXYETHYLTHIO)-SUBSTITUTED PHTHALOCYANINES
[CoIIIPc(ÿ2)] as indicated unequivocally by the resulting
electronic spectra. It was also reported that both the cathodic
and anodic portions of the peak corresponding to the
Co(III)/Co(II) couple were lost by the addition of hydroxide
CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) in the presence of 2.010ÿ3mol dmÿ3
TMACl. This spectrum is completely equivalent to that
recorded in the absence of TMACl and does not involve the
characteristic bands corresponding to the Co(III) and Co(I)
species. This observation indicates that disproportionation
(e.g. the conversion of CoIIPc(ÿ2) solution to a mixture of
[CoIPc(ÿ2)]ÿ and [CoIIIPc(ÿ2)]) does not take place in
the presence of chloride ions. However, a partial dispro-
portionation appears to occur in the solution adjacent to the
working electrode during the anodic sweep, because a
considerable decrease in the peak current of Va was
observed when the concentration of chloride ion was higher
than 8  10ÿ4mol dmÿ3 (Fig. 6).
Cyclic voltammetry of ZnIIOHETPc(ÿ2) in DMSO/TBAP
displays one-electron redox processes which appear to be
affected strongly by the presence of aggregated and non-
aggregated species. Some of the voltammetric peaks of this
complex seem to be split owing to the equilibrium between
these species. Thus care must be taken in distinguishing
redox potentials arising from mononuclear phthalocyanine
species and from aggregated species. A tentative mechan-
ism was suggested to explain this voltammetric behaviour.
In the case of CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) the splitting of the peaks
was not observed. This can be attributed to the fact that six-
coordinate metallophthalocyanine species do not aggregate
because they are kept apart by the axially bound ligands. On
the other hand, the redox properties of CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) are
inﬂuenced by different axial ligands attached to the metal
centre. Such binding appears to affect the observed redox
potentials. The voltammetric behaviour probably shows that
the solution of CoIIOHETPc(ÿ2) in DMSO/TBAP contains
several species in equilibrium having different Co(III)/
Co(II) oxidation potentials. More interesting behaviour
occurs if chloride ion is added. It was concluded that
chloride ion does not bind to either CoIIPc(ÿ2) or
[CoIIIPc(ÿ2)]. Solvent molecules or supporting electrolyte
anions play an important role in the redox properties of
